title: Charmed Life - Chapter 13 (Fragment 4)
text:
“I’m not,” said Cat. “I’m not any of those things. Mr. Saunders did it to give me a fright.”

“But Julia is a witch, isn’t she?” said the shrewd Janet. “What have I done to make her hate me so—or is it just general Gwendolenitis?”

Cat explained about the snakes.

“In which case I don’t blame her,” said Janet. “But it’s hard that she’s in the schoolroom at the moment brushing up her witchcraft, and here I am without a rag of a spell to defend myself with. You don’t know of a handy karate teacher, do you?”

“I never heard of one,” Cat said cautiously, wondering what karate might be.

“Oh well,” said Janet. “Chrestomanci’s a wonderfully fancy dresser, isn’t he?”

Cat laughed. “Wait till you see him in a dressing gown!”

“I hardly can. It must be something! Why is he so terrifying?”

“He just is,” said Cat.

“Yes,” said Janet. “He just is. When he saw the frog was Euphemia and went all mild and astonished like that, it froze the goose pimples on my back. I couldn’t have told him I wasn’t Gwendolen—not even under the most refined modern tortures—and that’s why I shall have to stick to you. Do you mind terribly?”

“Not at all,” said Cat. But he did rather. Janet could not have been more of a burden if she had been sitting on his shoulders with her legs wrapped around his chest. And to crown it all, it seemed as if there had been no need for his false confession. He took Janet to the ruins of the tree house because he wanted something else to think about. Janet was enchanted with it. She swung herself up into the horse chestnut to look at it, and Cat felt rather as you do when someone else gets into your railway carriage. “Be careful,” he called crossly.

There was a strong rending noise up in the tree. “Drat!” said Janet. “These are ridiculous clothes for climbing trees in.”

“Can’t you sew?” Cat called as he climbed up too.

“I despise it as female bondage,” said Janet. “Yes, I can, actually. And I’m going to have to. It was both petticoats.” She tested the creaking floor that was all that remained of the house and stood up on it, trailing two different colors of frill below the hem of Gwendolen’s dress. “You can see into the village from here. There’s a butcher’s cart just turning in to the Castle drive.”

Cat climbed up beside her and they watched the cart and the dappled horse pulling it.

“Don’t you have cars at all?” Janet asked. “Everyone has cars in my world.”

“Rich people do,” said Cat. “Chrestomanci sent his to meet us off the train.”

“And you have electric light,” said Janet. “But everything else is old-fashioned compared with my world. I suppose people can get what they want by witchcraft. Do you have factories, or long-playing records, or high-rise buildings, or television, or airplanes at all?”

“I don’t know what airplanes are,” said Cat. He had no idea what most of the other things were either, and he was bored with this talk.

Janet saw he was. She looked around for a change of subject and saw clusters of big green horse chestnut cases hanging all around them at the ends of the branches. The leaves there were already singed-looking around the edges, suggesting that the chestnuts could not be far off ripe. Janet edged out along a branch and tried to reach the nearest cluster of green cases. They bobbed at the tips of her fingers, just out of reach. “Oh, dachshunds!” she said. “They look almost ripe.”

vocabulary.definition: Very surprised or amazed.
vocabulary.example: Showing a gentle but strong surprise.

vocabulary.definition: Assign responsibility for a fault or wrong.
vocabulary.example: Not holding her responsible negatively for the circumstances.

vocabulary.definition: The state of being a slave or bound or restrained.
vocabulary.example: Feeling trapped by sewing as a traditional female task.

vocabulary.definition: To improve or refresh one's knowledge or skill in a particular area.
vocabulary.example: She is improving or revising her magical skills.

vocabulary.definition: A heavy load, especially a mental or emotional one.
vocabulary.example: She is an overwhelming responsibility or difficulty.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that deliberately avoids potential problems or dangers.
vocabulary.example: He spoke with care to avoid misunderstandings.

vocabulary.definition: A group of similar things or people positioned closely together.
vocabulary.example: Bunches of green horse chestnuts hanging together.

vocabulary.definition: A formal statement admitting that one is guilty or has done something.
vocabulary.example: An untruthful admission.

vocabulary.definition: Making a long, high sound when something moves or is moved.
vocabulary.example: The wooden floor making sounds when walked on.

vocabulary.definition: Marked with spots or rounded patches.
vocabulary.example: A horse with a spotted coat.

vocabulary.definition: To protect from harm or attack.
vocabulary.example: Use magic to protect oneself.

vocabulary.definition: Feel contempt or a deep repugnance for.
vocabulary.example: Strong dislike or hatred of sewing.

vocabulary.definition: Delighted or captivated.
vocabulary.example: She was very pleased or fascinated by it.

vocabulary.definition: Elaborate in structure or decoration; expensive or high quality.
vocabulary.example: Impressively dressed.

vocabulary.definition: A strip of gathered or pleated fabric used as decoration.
vocabulary.example: Decorative fabric trimming.

vocabulary.definition: A martial art developed in Japan focusing on striking.
vocabulary.example: Someone who can teach karate conveniently.

vocabulary.definition: An underskirt or slip worn beneath a skirt or dress.
vocabulary.example: Layers of clothing underneath a dress.

vocabulary.definition: A small piece of old cloth, or metaphorically something of little worth or power.
vocabulary.example: She has not even a minimal spell to defend herself.

vocabulary.definition: Elegant and cultured in appearance, manner, or taste.
vocabulary.example: Sophisticated or delicate in nature.

vocabulary.definition: Tearing something apart forcibly.
vocabulary.example: A loud noise like something tearing.

vocabulary.definition: Having or showing sharp powers of judgement; astute.
vocabulary.example: Janet, who is perceptive and clever in understanding the situation.

vocabulary.definition: Slightly burnt at the edges.
vocabulary.example: Leaves that look as if they are slightly burnt.

vocabulary.definition: A form of words used as a magical charm or incantation.
vocabulary.example: A magic formula for protection.

vocabulary.definition: Causing extreme fear.
vocabulary.example: He causes strong fear or intimidation.

vocabulary.definition: Severe pain inflicted on someone as punishment or to force them to do or say something.
vocabulary.example: Methods of causing extreme suffering.

vocabulary.definition: The practice of magic; the use of spells or supernatural powers.
vocabulary.example: Practising or learning magical arts.

vocabulary.definition: In a very good or impressive way.
vocabulary.example: Dresses with great style and flair.

vocabulary.definition: To be curious or doubtful about something.
vocabulary.example: He is unsure and curious about karate.
